zones.name: Autonomous Agent
zones.description: The hench package — a self-contained autonomous agent that picks PRD tasks, builds execution briefs, runs Claude API tool-use loops, and records run history.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

At 159 files with cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0.0, hench is the largest fully self-contained zone; this confirms the gateway pattern (rex-gateway.ts) is successfully encapsulating all cross-package imports so Louvain sees no external edges.

The agent/analysis/ subdirectory (adaptive.ts, review.ts, spin.ts) suggests a multi-phase reasoning pipeline; verifying these phases have unit tests is important given the cost of LLM round-trips in integration tests.

Zero external coupling is architecturally ideal for the execution tier, but it also means hench cannot be validated in isolation — any breaking change in rex types will only surface at runtime unless the gateway types are kept strictly in sync.
Zero coupling confirms the rex-gateway pattern is working correctly; all rex imports are concentrated in a single file, making the dependency surface explicit and auditable.

The agent/analysis/ pipeline (adaptive, review, spin) represents complex stateful LLM orchestration; each phase should have deterministic unit tests using mocked LLM responses to avoid flaky integration test suites.

At 159 files in a single zone, hench may benefit from internal zone decomposition (e.g. separating agent core, tool dispatch, and run storage) to make internal cohesion visible in future sourcevision analyses.
Zone "autonomous-agent" has files across 31 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

'autonomous-agent' is the only package-level zone named by function rather than by package name; every other package produces zones named with the package prefix (web-viewer, web-server, web-package-scaffold, rex-runtime-state). This breaks the implicit `{package}-{role}` convention and makes it impossible to identify hench's zone by scanning zone names for the string 'hench'.

Zero external coupling means breaking changes to hench's internal APIs (outside the rex-gateway) are completely invisible to static analysis and zone coupling scores — the only protection is the gateway's type contract, which is never automatically validated unless the TypeScript build is run with strict cross-package type checking enabled.

Rename zone 'autonomous-agent' to 'hench-agent' or 'hench-core' to align with the implicit {package}-{role} naming convention used by all other package-level zones (web-viewer, web-server, rex-runtime-state); the function-based name makes it impossible to locate hench's zone by scanning for the package name.

The rex-gateway.ts pattern provides type-level protection at the import boundary, but zero external coupling means no coupling metric will ever warn if hench's internal contracts drift from rex's exported types — add a CI step that runs tsc --noEmit across the hench→rex gateway interface specifically, ensuring type drift is caught before runtime rather than only during full builds.

zones.name: Design Documentation

insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
All 16 files are Markdown with no imports, so cohesion of 1.0 reflects isolation rather than semantic unity; the zone is a catch-all for docs/ directory contents.

Several files (memory-architecture.md, memory-improvements.md, memory-os-behavior.md, memory-risks-and-flaws.md) form a dense cluster on a single subsystem — consider grouping them under docs/memory/ to signal their relationship.

Level-system-reference.md and level-refactor-and-steward-plan.md suggest an in-progress architectural refactor; cross-referencing these docs from the PRD tasks they relate to would keep design intent traceable.
The memory subsystem is documented across four separate files; consolidating them under a docs/memory/ subdirectory would improve navigability without losing any content.
Design docs appear disconnected from the PRD task tree; linking doc files from the relevant rex tasks (or vice versa) would prevent design intent from diverging from implementation plans.

The 2026-03-03 refresh analysis file uses a date prefix, suggesting ad-hoc session notes rather than stable reference docs; a clear distinction between living reference docs and ephemeral analysis notes would help long-term maintainability.

The zone mixes stable reference documents (level-system-reference.md, prd-steward-vision.md) with dated session-analysis notes (2026-03-03-refresh-orchestration-memory-analysis.md) — these have opposite maintenance expectations: reference docs should be updated in-place, session notes should be archived or deleted. No naming convention or directory structure currently signals which category a file belongs to.

Four memory subsystem docs (memory-architecture.md, memory-improvements.md, memory-os-behavior.md, memory-risks-and-flaws.md) document a subsystem, but no corresponding source zone in the codebase carries a 'memory' identifier — the documentation cluster has drifted from the source zone naming, making it impossible to cross-reference docs to their implementation zones by name alone.

Establish a naming convention that distinguishes stable reference docs from ephemeral analysis notes — for example, prefix session notes with a date (already done for one file) and move them to docs/sessions/ while keeping reference docs at docs/ root; this prevents the zone from becoming an undifferentiated archive and makes retention decisions mechanical.

The four memory subsystem docs reference a domain that has no correspondingly named source zone; either rename the relevant source zone to include 'memory' or add a frontmatter/header in each doc that explicitly links to the source files it documents — without this cross-reference, the documentation will continue to drift as the implementation evolves.

zones.name: DOM Performance Monitoring
zones.description: Encapsulates runtime DOM performance measurement, pairing a low-level monitor utility with a React hook that surfaces metrics to the viewer.
insights: The utility/hook split is clean: dom-performance-monitor.ts owns measurement logic, use-dom-performance-monitor.ts owns React lifecycle — good separation of concerns within a small zone.
Low coupling (0.25) keeps this zone independently testable and replaceable if the performance strategy changes.
The single inbound import from web-viewer confirms this zone is consumed correctly as a focused leaf module rather than a shared utility.
Cohesion of 0.75 is solid for a 3-file zone; the test file, hook, and utility form a tight triangle with no extraneous files.
Zone has no entry points defined; if the hook is the intended public surface, marking it as an entry point would make the API contract explicit to tooling.

As one of two 'satellite' zones in this batch alongside orchestrator-e2e-tests, dom-performance-monitoring's single outbound edge (dom → web-viewer) and zero inbound edges from non-web-viewer zones confirm it is a true leaf with no hidden fans-out — changes here have a blast radius of exactly one zone.

dom-performance-monitoring → web-viewer is the only cross-zone edge for this zone; if performance monitoring ever needs to instrument zones other than web-viewer (e.g. web-server), a shared utility layer would be required to avoid duplicating measurement logic.

The zone's cohesion score (0.75) is partially inflated by the test file being one of only three zone members — stripping test files from the membership calculation would give a truer cohesion picture of the hook+utility pair alone; a two-file production zone would likely score lower and warrant a merge evaluation.

With only 2 production files (hook + utility), this zone is below the minimum viable zone size for independent lifecycle management; if no additional performance-monitoring files are planned, merging these two files into the web-viewer zone as an internal module would reduce zone overhead without losing the clean separation of concerns.

The zone's abbreviated identifier 'dom' (visible in cross-zone import labels as 'dom → web-viewer') does not match its full name 'dom-performance-monitoring', creating an ambiguous short-form that could be confused with DOM APIs or the browser DOM layer by readers unfamiliar with the zone graph.

The production files use 'monitor' (noun: dom-performance-monitor.ts, use-dom-performance-monitor.ts) while the zone name uses 'monitoring' (gerund: dom-performance-monitoring) — a subtle naming form mismatch that should be harmonized to one convention across zone ID, zone name, and filenames.

This zone is the only location in the monorepo where a React hook lives outside the web-viewer zone; all other hooks are co-located in web-viewer. This outlier placement is not inherently wrong but creates an implicit exception to an otherwise consistent convention that is nowhere documented.

Harmonize naming form: rename the zone to 'dom-performance-monitor' (noun) to match the actual filenames (dom-performance-monitor.ts, use-dom-performance-monitor.ts), or rename the files to use the gerund form — but resolve the noun/gerund split so zone ID, zone name, and filenames share one convention.

Document the exception: if keeping the hook outside web-viewer is intentional, add a comment in the hook file or a zone README noting that performance-monitoring hooks are isolated here by design. Without this, future contributors will not know whether the placement is a deliberate pattern or an oversight.

zones.name: Orchestrator E2E Tests
zones.description: End-to-end test suite covering the top-level CLI orchestration layer, validating delegation, config, init, CI pipeline, and architecture policy contracts.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling reflect ideal test-zone isolation — the suite imports nothing from sibling zones, relying only on spawned CLI processes.
Coverage spans the full orchestration surface (delegation, config, dev, errors, init, CI, usage, sync) — this zone acts as the integration contract for the entire n-dx CLI.
Architecture-policy tests in this zone enforce structural rules (e.g. no circular deps) at the process level, which is a strong safeguard for the four-tier dependency hierarchy.
Zero coupling is the correct design for an E2E suite — tests that import production modules directly would blur the boundary between unit and E2E layers.
The 14-file suite covers cli-delegation and architecture-policy as distinct concerns; ensure architecture-policy.test.js assertions are kept up to date as the gateway module pattern evolves.

The orchestrator-e2e-tests zone validates CLI delegation paths (ndx start, ndx work, etc.) via spawned processes, but HTTP transport session management and MCP protocol compliance for the web package are not exercised at this level — those paths would require a dedicated integration test zone consuming the live server.
Zero inbound coupling to orchestrator-e2e-tests means this zone has a blast radius of zero on all production code — it can be restructured, deleted, or split without any source-level consequences.

The e2e suite covers CLI delegation but not HTTP/MCP session lifecycle; as the web package's HTTP transport matures, a separate e2e zone targeting the live server would close this coverage gap without violating the zero-coupling constraint of the existing orchestrator suite.

The 1.0 cohesion score in this zone almost certainly reflects Louvain grouping all zero-outbound-coupling files together rather than genuine import inter-dependency between individual test files; each test file in this zone is likely a standalone module with no imports from sibling test files — the cohesion metric is a topological artifact, not a semantic signal here.

The suite acts as a structural mirror of the CLI command surface (one file per ndx command area); additions to the CLI surface should be accompanied by a corresponding test file in this zone to maintain coverage parity — the absence of a file for a given command is a visible gap in this structure.

There is no test file covering the `ndx sync` command (sync-with-remote adapter path) visible in the listed files, despite sync being a documented ndx orchestration command; if this gap exists, the CLI delegation contract for sync is unverified at the E2E layer.

architecture-policy.test.js validates static structural properties (circular dependency absence, gateway module rules) and belongs to a different test category than the CLI delegation tests that surround it; grouping them in the same zone conflates behavioral E2E testing with architectural linting, making it harder to run structural checks independently in CI.

The orchestrator-e2e-tests zone covers all ndx CLI commands via spawned processes but has zero coverage of the MCP HTTP transport session lifecycle — the recommended transport path (HTTP) is entirely untested at the E2E layer while the legacy path (stdio) is implicitly tested via spawned CLI processes. This gap grows as HTTP becomes the primary integration surface.

The 14-file suite has a structural one-file-per-command mapping (cli-ci, cli-config, cli-delegation, cli-dev, cli-errors, cli-init, cli-orchestration, architecture-policy), but the remaining 6 unlisted files break this symmetry — their naming and coverage scope are opaque from the zone manifest alone, which weakens the suite's role as a visible CLI contract mirror.

Extract architecture-policy.test.js into a dedicated 'architecture-tests' zone or directory (e.g. tests/architecture/) separate from e2e/. Structural enforcement tests have different triggers (run on any PR), different failure modes (static analysis vs runtime), and different audiences than CLI delegation E2E tests — co-location obscures both purposes.

Add a dedicated e2e test file for the MCP HTTP transport layer (e.g. cli-mcp-http.test.js) that starts the ndx server, establishes a session via the Streamable HTTP transport, and exercises at least one rex and one sourcevision MCP tool call end-to-end. HTTP transport is now the documented-recommended path but has no E2E coverage in this zone.

llm-types.ts is generically named in a zone scoped to provider concerns — if this file contains package-wide shared types (token structs, config shapes) rather than provider-only types, its placement in provider-core is the structural root cause of the 3 reverse imports: core holds types that utilities need, so utilities import core to get them rather than importing a neutral types zone.

provider-session.ts implies stateful session management at the transport layer, but auth credentials live in auth.ts in llm-cli-utilities with no visible contract between the two — session construction likely requires auth context, making the session↔auth coupling implicit and untraceable across zone boundaries.

Rename codex-cli-provider.ts to follow the transport-first naming pattern used by api-provider and cli-provider — e.g. codex-provider.ts or openai-cli-provider.ts depending on whether the file abstraction is vendor or transport. Mixing both in the filename breaks the zone's naming convention and will compound as more vendors are added.

Audit llm-types.ts to determine whether it holds provider-scoped or package-wide types. If it contains types shared with llm-cli-utilities (project dir shapes, token structs, provider config interfaces), move those types to a new zero-dependency types module — this is likely the direct cause of the 3 reverse imports and fixing the placement fixes the cycle structurally.

The zone graph contains zero shared-UI or shared-assets zone, forcing every view or panel that needs a common presentational primitive (e.g., BrandedHeader) to import from web-dashboard-core and create a cross-zone coupling edge. Introducing a single shared-ui zone as a leaf with no outbound production imports would eliminate multiple coupling edges simultaneously and provide a clear home for any future shared primitives without polluting the core zone.

All five zones examined in this pass follow kebab-case naming, but three zone names ('web-dashboard-core', 'prd-input-panels', 'viewer-shell') embed structural or locational information that becomes misleading as the zone's actual content diverges from its name — a sign that zone names were set at detection time and never revisited as the zones evolved. Establishing a convention of naming zones by role (e.g., 'spa-bootstrap', 'prd-authoring', 'web-runtime') rather than by structure or location would make names more durable.

The dom-performance-monitoring zone is the sole location in the entire monorepo where a React hook lives outside of the web-viewer zone. If web-viewer is the intended canonical home for all React hooks, this should be documented as a convention and the dom zone evaluated for absorption. If hooks outside web-viewer are allowed, the convention needs to be defined to prevent ad-hoc hook zones proliferating alongside future feature work.

The orchestrator-e2e-tests zone mixes two structurally different test categories — behavioral CLI delegation tests and static architectural enforcement (architecture-policy.test.js) — under one zone. These have different CI triggers, failure modes, and audiences. Separating them into distinct zones or directories would make architectural enforcement visible and independently executable without requiring the full E2E suite to run.

The MCP HTTP transport path — now the documented-recommended integration surface — has no representation in the orchestrator-e2e-tests zone. The zone tests CLI delegation via spawned processes but not HTTP session establishment, tool dispatch over Streamable HTTP, or session teardown. As HTTP becomes the primary transport, this gap leaves the highest-traffic integration path entirely unverified at the E2E layer.

Across all four enrichment passes, every warning-or-above finding originates from the web package; hench, rex, sourcevision, llm-client, and the orchestration layer have produced zero warning-level findings. This asymmetry suggests the web package would benefit from a dedicated architectural review cadence (e.g. a weekly CI job that re-runs zone analysis and fails on new bidirectional imports) separate from the general CI pipeline that covers all packages equally.

The dom-performance-monitoring and orchestrator-e2e-tests zones both lack entry point declarations, but for opposite reasons: dom has a clear intended entry point (the hook) that is undeclared, while orchestrator-e2e-tests has no meaningful entry point by design (test files are not consumed by other zones). Tooling that treats all entry-point-free zones identically will misclassify these two structurally different cases — entry point absence should be annotated with intent (intentional isolation vs. missing declaration) to prevent false positives in zone health reports.

Establish and document a canonical zone naming convention (e.g. `{package}-{role}`) and apply it retroactively to 'autonomous-agent' (→ 'hench-agent') and any other zones using non-standard naming patterns; this makes the zone map self-documenting and allows engineers to identify a zone's owning package by name alone.

The cross-zone import graph uses bare zone IDs (e.g. 'web') that differ from zone display names (e.g. 'web-package-scaffold'); standardize zone IDs to match display names or add an alias table in the sourcevision output so import graph consumers can resolve IDs without a separate lookup.

No CODEOWNERS file exists at the repo root; without ownership assignments, all architectural findings from zone analysis (bidirectional coupling, mis-zoned files, retention policy gaps) have no assigned party to action them — add a CODEOWNERS file that maps each package directory and key zone to a responsible maintainer.

Establish a documented convention distinguishing unit, integration, and architecture-guard tests by directory tier (tests/unit/, tests/integration/, tests/architecture/); boundary-check.test.ts and ws-health-integration.test.ts are both currently misplaced under tests/unit/server/, creating a systematic misclassification that affects zone coupling scores and CI tier filtering.

Create a shared-ui leaf zone (e.g., packages/web/src/viewer/shared-ui/) for purely presentational primitives like BrandedHeader/logos.ts; this single change would eliminate at least one direction of the prd-input-panels↔web-dashboard-core bidirectional coupling and provide a stable, convention-backed location for future shared components.

Adopt a zone naming policy that encodes role rather than structure: prefer 'spa-bootstrap' over 'viewer-shell', 'prd-authoring' over 'prd-input-panels', 'web-runtime' over 'web-dashboard-core'. Zone names chosen at Louvain detection time tend to reflect topology rather than intent and should be reviewed whenever a zone's responsibilities are clarified.

Introduce a zone-type annotation convention (e.g. a 'role' field in zone metadata) that distinguishes: production-leaf, production-hub, test-e2e, test-unit, architecture-enforcement, artifact. This would immediately resolve the recurring misclassification of orchestrator-e2e-tests (cohesion 1.0 as topological artifact), dom-performance-monitoring (micro production-leaf), and .rex/docs (data artifact zones) — all of which produce misleading health scores under the current single-metric system.

Add a CI gate that fails if any new bidirectional import pair is introduced in the web package's zone graph. The three existing bidirectional pairs (web-viewer↔web-server, web-viewer↔panel, web-viewer↔web-package-scaffold) are known debt; a CI gate would prevent the pattern from expanding while the existing cases are resolved incrementally.

web-package-scaffold has both degraded cohesion (0.79) and non-zero coupling (0.21), making it the only structurally fragile zone in this batch; the root cause is that three unrelated file categories (debugging artifacts, production utilities, build config) are co-located — splitting elapsed-time.ts and task-audit.ts into web-viewer and removing the debugging markdown files would raise cohesion and reduce coupling simultaneously without any logic changes.

Mutual imports with web-dashboard-core suggest the panels reach into viewer state rather than receiving it as props/events; a unidirectional data flow pattern would improve testability and zone independence.

elapsed-time.ts and task-audit.ts are consumed by web-viewer but live in this residual zone; if they are stable shared utilities they should be co-located with the components that use them to reduce cross-zone hops.

Including a ws-health-integration.test.ts and a boundary-check.test.ts alongside unit tests shows good layered testing strategy for real-time infrastructure.

The e2e suite covers CLI delegation but not HTTP/MCP session lifecycle; as the web package's HTTP transport matures, a separate e2e zone targeting the live server would close this coverage gap without violating the zero-coupling constraint of the existing orchestrator suite.

analysis.ts (page/view) co-located with panel components inside the same zone blends routing and component responsibilities; extracting the view into web-dashboard-core or a dedicated views zone would likely eliminate one direction of the bidirectional coupling with the core.
Coupling-to-cohesion ratio of ~0.60 is the highest in this batch — external pull on this zone is nearly as strong as its internal coherence, signaling an unstable boundary.

Zero coupling in both directions (no imports to or from any zone) is the expected and correct pattern for an HTML bootstrap shell — no action needed and serves as a reference for static asset zone hygiene.

dom-performance-monitoring → web-viewer is the only cross-zone edge for this zone; if performance monitoring ever needs to instrument zones other than web-viewer (e.g. web-server), a shared utility layer would be required to avoid duplicating measurement logic.
web-viewer imports from web-unit 6 times — the largest single cross-zone edge in the graph. If web-unit is a test-support zone, this is a critical production→test boundary violation.
web-viewer → web (2 imports) pulls scaffold or config-layer code into the viewer at runtime; confirm these are not test utilities or startup-only helpers leaking into the production module graph.

web-viewer → web (2 imports) combined with web → web-viewer (4 imports) means the scaffold zone both consumes and is consumed by the viewer core — a layering inversion where the entry/config layer depends on the application layer and vice versa.
Purely unidirectional dependency flow (consumed by web-dashboard-core, emits nothing outward) — reference pattern for how satellite zones should relate to the core zone.

With only 2 production files (hook + utility), this zone is below the minimum viable zone size for independent lifecycle management; if no additional performance-monitoring files are planned, merging these two files into the web-viewer zone as an internal module would reduce zone overhead without losing the clean separation of concerns.

There is no test file covering the `ndx sync` command (sync-with-remote adapter path) visible in the listed files, despite sync being a documented ndx orchestration command; if this gap exists, the CLI delegation contract for sync is unverified at the E2E layer.

analysis.ts is co-located in prd-input-panels but imports BrandedHeader from web-dashboard-core (logos.ts), creating the prd-input-panels → web-dashboard-core coupling direction. BrandedHeader is a pure presentational component with no domain logic; it should be extracted into a shared UI primitives location accessible to all viewer zones without creating cross-zone coupling.

usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts documents that it 'Broadcasts a WebSocket event so the dashboard can react' but does not import websocket infrastructure directly — the WebSocket dependency is injected at the call site, hiding a runtime coupling that the static zone graph cannot measure. This invisible dependency means the zone's coupling score of 0.38 understates its true runtime coupling.

physics.ts is classified as part of the 'web-unit' sub-zone due to Louvain co-placement with its test files, but it is a pure production module (Barnes-Hut quad-tree engine extracted from graph.ts). This misclassification generates a false 'production-to-test' coupling alarm; relocate physics.ts adjacent to graph.ts in the viewer/graph/ directory to align its zone membership with its actual consumers.

elapsed-time.ts and task-audit.ts are misplaced in the scaffold zone but are primarily consumed by web-viewer; they should be co-located with their consumers to eliminate the layering inversion these files cause.

web-package-scaffold imports from web-viewer (4 edges), inverting the expected config-layer→application-layer dependency direction; bootstrap and config files must not depend on the application zone they initialize.

boundary-check.test.ts enforces a package-wide server/viewer import boundary for all of src/ but is grouped into the websocket-infrastructure zone, making it appear to be a WebSocket contract test. This misplacement means the boundary enforcement test is invisible when reviewing the web package's overall test strategy and would not be found by developers looking for architectural guard tests.

Establish a naming convention that distinguishes stable reference docs from ephemeral analysis notes — for example, prefix session notes with a date (already done for one file) and move them to docs/sessions/ while keeping reference docs at docs/ root; this prevents the zone from becoming an undifferentiated archive and makes retention decisions mechanical.

The four memory subsystem docs reference a domain that has no correspondingly named source zone; either rename the relevant source zone to include 'memory' or add a frontmatter/header in each doc that explicitly links to the source files it documents — without this cross-reference, the documentation will continue to drift as the implementation evolves.

Document the exception: if keeping the hook outside web-viewer is intentional, add a comment in the hook file or a zone README noting that performance-monitoring hooks are isolated here by design. Without this, future contributors will not know whether the placement is a deliberate pattern or an oversight.

Harmonize naming form: rename the zone to 'dom-performance-monitor' (noun) to match the actual filenames (dom-performance-monitor.ts, use-dom-performance-monitor.ts), or rename the files to use the gerund form — but resolve the noun/gerund split so zone ID, zone name, and filenames share one convention.

Add a dedicated e2e test file for the MCP HTTP transport layer (e.g. cli-mcp-http.test.js) that starts the ndx server, establishes a session via the Streamable HTTP transport, and exercises at least one rex and one sourcevision MCP tool call end-to-end. HTTP transport is now the documented-recommended path but has no E2E coverage in this zone.

Extract architecture-policy.test.js into a dedicated 'architecture-tests' zone or directory (e.g. tests/architecture/) separate from e2e/. Structural enforcement tests have different triggers (run on any PR), different failure modes (static analysis vs runtime), and different audiences than CLI delegation E2E tests — co-location obscures both purposes.

Add unit tests for analyze-panel.ts and proposal-editor.ts to match the coverage level already established for batch-import-panel.ts and smart-add-input.ts; uneven intra-zone coverage creates a false confidence signal when reviewing the zone's test health.
Rename the zone from 'prd-input-panels' to 'prd-authoring' to accurately reflect its mixed panel+view membership and to avoid implying all members are input widgets.

Rename 'project-root-config' to 'orchestration-root' or 'monorepo-root' to accurately reflect that it contains active orchestration scripts (ci.js, cli.js, web.js) and documentation alongside configuration files — the current name implies passive config and misleads contributors about the zone's operational role.

Document or enforce a two-tier retention policy within .rex/: persistent files (prd.json, config.json, archive.json) should be committed and backed up, while ephemeral files (pending-proposals.json, acknowledged-findings.json, execution-log*.jsonl) should be gitignored or pruned automatically — mixing them in one directory with no policy distinction creates operational risk if someone treats ephemeral state as authoritative.

Split task-usage-tracking into two single-responsibility units: a domain zone for incremental-task-usage.ts (task event accumulation) and absorb usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts into web-dashboard-core as an operational infrastructure concern. This resolves the layer violation and eliminates the zone's weak cohesion score.
Rename zone 'viewer-shell' to 'spa-bootstrap' or 'app-shell' to describe its bootstrap role rather than repeating its directory path prefix; zone names should convey purpose, not location.

Move ws-health-integration.test.ts from tests/unit/server/ to tests/integration/server/ to match its name and actual testing tier; leaving it under unit/ makes CI tier filtering incorrect and misleads reviewers about the test's scope.
